rationale: The citing paper uses Bollinger et al. (2021) for a specific empirical claim: nutrition labeling plus promotion produced only modest improvements in healthier purchases. This evidence is used to frame and interpret the paper's own main result that higher minimum wages increase calories but do not improve average dietary quality, positioning the study against a supply-side nudge literature. Although the citation is not used for method or data reuse, it plays a meaningful role in conceptual positioning and discussion of contribution.
relation_description: The citing paper draws on the cited paper as comparative prior evidence that label-based education interventions have limited effects on nutrition outcomes. It uses that result to contextualize its demand-side policy findings on minimum wage changes and consumer nutrition.
